metadata.working_title: Leveraging Permutation Testing to Assess Confidence in Positive-Unlabeled Learning Applied to High-Dimensional Biological Datasets

abstract_sketch.problem: Positive-Unlabeled (PU) learning methods classify samples when only a small set of known positives and a large unlabeled set are available, but validating predictions without negative examples is a major barrier in biological applications.
abstract_sketch.gap: Prior PU learning evaluations rely on ground-truth negative labels (accuracy, F1, MCC) that are unavailable in real-world biomedical contexts; no prior study has used permutation testing to evaluate confidence in PU classifications.
abstract_sketch.approach: We combine a k-fold spy-positive technique with a transductive PU bagging SVM-RBF classifier and compare Explicit Positive Recall (EPR) and Mean Bagging Score (MBS) for the known-positive set under actual vs. permuted P/U labels using Welch's t-test, Cliff's delta with 95% CI, and p-values from a z-score.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Across nine synthetic datasets spanning three class separations (0, 1, 2) and three true-negative proportions (10%, 30%, 50%), the Wisconsin Diagnostic Breast Cancer (WDBC) dataset, a BRCA (n=300) vs. LUAD (n=141) PANCAN subset, and the Lakhashe et al. vaccine dataset (n=36), significant differences between actual and permuted scores align with U-AUC ranging from 1.00 (easy) to 0.54 (hard). The z-score p-value remains stable across 30, 100, and 500 permutation repetitions, while Welch's t-test is destabilized by increasing permutations; Cliff's delta preserves the 'large' boundary (>0.474) under actual labels.
abstract_sketch.impact: Permutation testing can serve as a gatekeeper for PU learning confidence in settings lacking ground-truth negatives, enabling practitioners to distinguish reliable from unreliable model outputs.

sections.title: Background
content_bullets: High-dimensional (p>=n) biological datasets suffer from the curse of dimensionality; strategies include regularization, cross-validation, and permutation testing.
Permutation testing scrambles labels to create a null distribution against which observed model performance is compared.
PU learning addresses the common biomedical setting in which only positive examples are known and the unlabeled set contains both positives and negatives.
Major PU learning categories are Reliable Negative Selection and Base Classifier Adaptation; PU Bagging is an ensemble strategy that is robust to classifier choice.

Existing PU evaluations use accuracy/F1/MCC, which require true negatives. EPR has been proposed but whether good positive-example recall is a surrogate for overall prediction quality is context-dependent.
We propose combining the spy-positive technique with permutation testing to evaluate known-positive set scores against a null distribution, without requiring ground-truth negatives.

subsections.title: Generating a null distribution of positive-class scores via spy folds and label permutation
content_bullets: Context: in PU learning there is no reference distribution for positive-set scores when true negatives are unavailable.

Approach: each known-positive sample is placed into a spy fold and scored by PU bagging; scores are aggregated by EPR and MBS across 30 repeated k-fold splits (k=5), then compared with distributions obtained after 30/100/500 label permutations (Fig. 1).
Finding: the pipeline yields matched actual and permuted score distributions for each dataset and scoring method.
Interpretation: this design enables a principled hypothesis test for whether KP samples carry separable signal without negative labels.

subsections.title: Evaluating confidence on high-dimensional synthetic datasets
content_bullets: Context: nine synthetic datasets with class separations of 0 (negative control), 1 (medium), and 2 (high), and true-negative proportions of 10%, 30%, and 50% (200 features, 200 subjects; 30% informative, 70% redundant).

Approach: compute U-AUC between PU bagging scores for the unlabeled set and ground-truth labels; compare EPR and MBS for actual vs. permuted positives using Welch's t-test, Cliff's delta with 95% CI, and z-score p-values (Fig. 2, Fig. 3).

Finding: at 50% TN, U-AUC varied from 1.00 (excellent) to 0.64 (near random) with class separation. At 10% TN, U-AUC fell to a range of 0.88 (excellent) to 0.54 (no discrimination). At class separation = 1 and 10% TN, actual-vs-permuted EPR and MBS became not significantly different; at class separation = 0, significance was generally absent as expected.
Interpretation: EPR was somewhat less susceptible to false positive observations; the null-distribution comparison correctly identifies the regime in which PU scores should not be trusted.

subsections.title: Evaluating confidence on real-world biological benchmarks
content_bullets: Context: WDBC (400 samples drawn from 569 instances at 50%, 30%, and 10.8% TN), PANCAN BRCA/LUAD (TP n=300, TN n=141, 325 PCs retaining 95% variance), and the Lakhashe et al. vaccine dataset (36 Rhesus Macaques, TN group L n=36) with KP fractions of 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%.
Approach: compute U-AUC and compare EPR and MBS between actual and permuted labels (Welch's t-test with ****p<0.0001 thresholds), z-score, and Cliff's delta (Fig. 4).

Finding: for all three datasets, high U-AUC coincided with significant separation between actual and permuted score distributions across NKP. For WDBC, even when EPR and MBS approached 0.5, U-AUC remained >0.9. For the small Lakhashe dataset, increasing NKP produced greater gains in permuted than actual scores, yet distributions remained distinct with a large effect size.
Interpretation: across varied real-world regimes, permutation-based comparisons provide confidence in KP set quality without requiring ground-truth negatives.

subsections.title: Stability of comparison metrics across permutation repetitions
content_bullets: Context: permutation testing increases compute cost linearly in the number of permutations; practitioners need guidance on how many permutations suffice.

Approach: apply the pipeline to the Lakhashe dataset and a dimension-matched synthetic dataset (n=100, p=200, 30% TN; KP=20 -> U-AUC=0.696, KP=40 -> U-AUC=0.733), varying permutation count across 30, 100, and 500 and computing Cliff's delta, z-score p-value, and Welch's t-test p-value (Fig. 5).

Finding: Cliff's delta estimates preserved the 'large' boundary (>0.474) for actual labels across permutation counts; z-score p-values were stable across permutation counts in both high- and low-significance regimes; Welch's t-test p-values drifted monotonically with permutation count, indicating inflation artifacts.
Interpretation: z-score comparisons are the preferred metric, consistent with the conceptual one-versus-many structure of comparing a single actual label set to many permuted sets.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Paragraph 1: summarize the novel contribution: permutation testing applied inside a PU bagging + spy-positive pipeline, yielding a null distribution for positive-set scores.
Paragraph 2: empirical behaviour across class separation, TP/TN imbalance, and KP fractions; methodology distinguishes reliable from unreliable inference.

Paragraph 3: metric choice: z-score is robust to permutation count, Welch's t-test is inflated by large permutation counts, Cliff's delta preserves effect-size category; MBS usually shows lower variance than EPR.
Paragraph 4: limitations: 10% TN regime can yield false negative signals even when U-AUC is acceptable; current approach cannot pinpoint whether class overlap or imbalance is responsible.

Paragraph 5: future work: improved PU algorithms for extreme imbalance, more informative KP-set scoring that captures probability distributions, investigation of the impact of k in k-fold spy positive splits.

sections.title: Conclusions
content_bullets: Permutation testing serves as a no-information-rate baseline for PU learning, analogous to its role in supervised ML.
Across varied class-label ratios and class separations, the methodology provides an initial gatekeeper for PU classification deployments in real-world biomedical settings.

subsections.title: Datasets
content_bullets: Synthetic datasets (scikit-learn): 200 features, 200 subjects per dataset; 30% informative, 70% redundant features (random linear combinations); 3 separations x 3 TN proportions = 9 datasets; small variant n=100, p=200 for permutation-count ablation.
WDBC: 30 numeric features across 10 characteristics; 400 subjects sampled from 569 with 50%, 30%, and 10.8% TN; Malign relabelled Class 0 (TN), Benign relabelled Class 1 (TP).
BRCA/LUAD from PANCAN: TP BRCA n=300, TN LUAD n=141; PCA to 325 PCs retaining 95% variance.
Lakhashe et al.: 36 Rhesus Macaques, three regimens M/K/L; group L defined as TN (n=36).

subsections.title: Analysis Pipeline
content_bullets: K-fold spy positive (k=5): KP split into 5 folds, one fold reassigned to U at a time; 30 different fold partitions per KP set.
PU Bagging (Mordelet et al.): bootstrapped unlabeled samples labelled as negative, 100-time repeated bagging; SVM-RBF classifier with scikit-learn defaults.
Scoring: EPR = fraction of KP with bagging score >0.5; MBS = mean class-1 probability for KP.
Permutation: KP/U labels randomly shuffled; 30, 100, and 500 replicates tested.
Statistics: Welch's t-test, Cliff's delta with 95% CI (large boundary = 0.474), upper-tailed one-sample z-test (z = (mu - mu_0) / sigma) comparing actual mean to permuted distribution.
